incident.title: Tier III Data Center — Li-Ion UPS Thermal Runaway

act_00.title: Baseline Reality
act_00.subtitle: System state before the event
act_00.summary: Site Alpha is a Tier III colocation facility hosting critical government and public services infrastructure. The facility operates 647 services across 120 server racks with a total hosted data volume of 858 TB — none replicated offsite. UPS Battery Array A consists of 384 Li-ion modules installed 11.1 years ago, exceeding the manufacturer's rated 10-year lifespan by 13 months. Three modules showing elevated temperatures — flagged in BMS but not escalated. Battery-to-server proximity: 60cm (below NFPA 855 recommended separation). No off-gas detection deployed in battery room.

signals.text: BMS reports 3 modules with elevated temperature — within alert threshold but trending upward
Scheduled battery inspection postponed — technician reassigned to unrelated work order
Module temperatures stable at elevated baseline — no automatic escalation triggered
Shift handover — elevated battery temps not included in verbal briefing
One module voltage reading diverges from array average — logged, not flagged
Routine server maintenance begins in adjacent hall — normal traffic

act_01.title: Risk Accumulation
act_01.subtitle: Conditions that enabled the event
act_01.summary: Over the preceding 30 days, multiple risk factors converged without recognition. Battery array exceeded rated lifespan — no replacement scheduled. Job Safety Analysis not completed for battery room work. No hot work permit issued. Battery State of Charge not verified before scheduled disconnection. Technician lacked Li-ion-specific hazmat training or third-party certification. Battery-to-server proximity: 60cm (below NFPA 855 recommended separation). No off-gas detection deployed in battery room.

signals.text: Battery array passes rated lifespan — no replacement scheduled
BMS alert threshold raised by operator to reduce alarm fatigue
Quarterly fire suppression inspection delayed — rescheduled indefinitely
Server expansion reduces battery-to-rack clearance below recommended minimum
Job Safety Analysis for battery maintenance — NOT COMPLETED
Hot work permit for adjacent electrical panel — NOT ISSUED
Off-gas detection budget request denied — classified as non-essential

act_02.title: State Change
act_02.subtitle: The triggering event and initial response
act_02.summary: At T-0, a single Li-ion module in UPS Battery Array A enters thermal runaway. Voltage spike detected by BMS. Within 90 seconds, cascade propagation begins across adjacent modules. Aspirating detector triggers at T+2min. Spot detector confirms at T+3min. Building alarm activates. Clean agent system deploys at T+5min — agent disperses but does not arrest thermal runaway (designed for Class A/B, not Li-ion thermal decomposition). CO₂ system deploys at T+8min — cooling effect insufficient against exothermic chain reaction. EPO activated at T+10min — partial failure: generator auto-starts, re-energizing partial circuits.

signals.text: Module 247 — voltage spike 4.2V → 5.1V — BMS thermal runaway flag
Module 247 temperature exceeds 130°C — adjacent modules 246, 248 entering thermal stress
Cascade propagation confirmed — 12 modules in thermal runaway
Aspirating detector triggers — pre-alarm smoke detection in server hall
Spot detector array confirms — battery room smoke density exceeds threshold
Clean agent system deploys — agent concentration achieved but thermal runaway continues
CO₂ system deploys — minimal cooling effect, does not reach thermal runaway threshold
EPO activated — mains disconnected but generator auto-starts, re-energizing partial circuits
Fire department dispatched — hazmat team requested for lithium-ion event

act_03.title: Cascade & Constraint
act_03.subtitle: Why the situation escalated beyond control
act_03.summary: Following initial suppression failure, the fire cascaded through 384 battery modules generating toxic hydrogen fluoride gas and temperatures exceeding 160°C. Clean agent and CO₂ proved chemically incompatible with Li-ion thermal runaway — neither could interrupt the self-sustaining exothermic decomposition. Water suppression was available but ruled out to protect adjacent server equipment — a decision that prioritized hardware over containment. Fire department operations lasted 22 hours with 101 personnel and 22 vehicles. All 647 hosted services went offline. 858 TB of data destroyed with zero offsite backup. Recovery assessment at 2 weeks: 24% of services partially restored.

signals.text: Battery room temperature exceeds 160°C — full array in thermal runaway
HF gas detected in adjacent corridors — evacuation zone expanded
Clean agent exhausted — no refill capacity, system cannot re-engage
Fire department establishes command — confirms Li-ion battery fire with toxic off-gassing
Fire partition holds but heat transfer through cable penetrations to server hall
Server hall temperatures reach critical — rack thermal shutdown begins
CRAH units offline — cooling loop compromised
All 647 services confirmed OFFLINE — national service disruption declared
Water suppression authorized after server loss confirmed — fire partially contained
Defensive operations continue — adjacent floors protected
Fire intensity decreasing — overhaul operations begin
Fire declared under control — hazmat decontamination begins
858 TB confirmed destroyed — zero offsite backup existed

act_04.title: Post-Event Intel
act_04.subtitle: Root causes, lessons, and recommended countermeasures
act_04.summary: Post-event analysis identifies 11 root causes across three categories: Technical (suppression chemistry mismatch, detection gap, battery end-of-life), Procedural (JSA failure, hot work permit gap, SoC verification skip, training deficit), and Architectural (proximity violation, single-point data failure, EPO logic error, insufficient passive barriers). The fundamental suppression gap — inability to address Li-ion thermal runaway with conventional clean agent or CO₂ — is the primary technical finding. Encapsulator agent technology (NFPA 18A class) with micelle mist delivery (NFPA 750) addresses all three hazard vectors: flammability, explosivity, and toxicity.

root_causes.finding: Clean agent system designed for Class A/B fires — chemically cannot arrest Li-ion thermal decomposition
CO₂ cooling insufficient to interrupt exothermic chain reaction above 80°C self-heating threshold
No off-gas detection — missed 25-minute early warning window before visible smoke
Battery array operated 13 months beyond manufacturer rated lifespan
Job Safety Analysis not completed for battery maintenance work
Hot work permit not issued for adjacent electrical panel work
Battery State of Charge not verified before scheduled work
60cm battery-to-server proximity below NFPA 855 recommended separation
858 TB hosted data with zero offsite replication or backup
EPO logic did not account for generator auto-start — created re-energization hazard

what_changed: 647 services lost — national-scale disruption affecting 50M+ citizens
858 TB of government data destroyed with no backup
Critical infrastructure facility rendered non-operational for 22+ hours
Emergency services degraded during event window
what_it_means: Conventional clean agent and CO₂ suppression cannot arrest Li-ion thermal runaway
The fundamental suppression gap in data center battery rooms remains unaddressed by standard systems
Off-gas detection provides a 25-minute early warning window that was entirely absent
Battery end-of-life management is a critical fire prevention control

immediate: Deploy encapsulator agent micelle mist suppression in all battery rooms
Install off-gas detection (HF, CO, VOC) with automated BMS integration
Enforce mandatory JSA completion for all battery room work
next: Migrate to LFP chemistry (lower thermal runaway risk) at next replacement cycle
Implement multi-region data failover with 3-2-1 backup strategy
Install dedicated suppression integration system for encapsulator delivery
Review and correct EPO logic to prevent generator re-energization
watchlist: NFPA 855 2026 edition — Large-Scale Fire Test requirements for BESS
ISO 9001:2026 revision for quality management updates
EPA PFAS regulations — verify suppression agents are fluorine-free
Monitor Li-ion fire incident patterns across APAC data center sector

hct_solution.description: Encapsulator agent delivered via water mist nozzle technology. Purpose-built for Li-ion thermal runaway in occupied, electronics-dense environments. Addresses all three hazard vectors simultaneously.
three_level_mitigation: FLAMMABILITY — Encapsulates fuel molecules, interrupts free radical chain reaction, absorbs 10x more heat than plain water
EXPLOSIVITY — Renders off-gases non-ignitable through micelle encapsulation of volatile organic compounds
TOXICITY — Reduces HF and other toxic emission concentrations through chemical binding
key_advantages: 3x cooling capacity vs. plain water — faster thermal runaway arrest
Safe for occupied spaces — non-toxic, no oxygen displacement
Electronics-compatible — no residue damage to adjacent server equipment
Fluorine-free — compliant with emerging EPA PFAS regulations
Integrates with existing sprinkler infrastructure via proportioning systems
